data.title: Week Ahead: SCOTUS Decision On Tariffs? 8 Fed Officials Speak As The Market Discounts Almost 65 Bp Of Cuts This Year
data.description:
Last week began with the LDP's stunning victory in Japan. However, rather than sell-off as the market expected, the yen and JGBs rallied. It appears that the yen's use as a levered funding leg for carry trades had already been adjusted. At the start of the week, reports circulated that Chinese officials were encouraging local financial institutions to de-risk from US Treasuries. Yet despite it and the better-than-expected January US employment data, the 10-year Treasury yield fell nearly 15 bp last week, the most in five months. The yield slipped below 4.05% ahead of the weekend, the lowest in more than two months. The greenback fell against the G10 currencies last week, but consolidation seemed to be the main feature. At the end of the week, the futures market discounted nearly 65 bp of Fed cuts this year, up from almost 56 bp at the end of the previous week. This is to say, the derivatives market is discounting two Fed cuts fully and about a 60% chance of a third.

Although it is not in economic diaries, February 20 is a "decision day" for the US Supreme Court. It is possible that the court delivers its decision about the president's use of emergency powers to levy broad tariffs. Judging from the event markets, most expect the court to rule against the administration. As we have noted, some companies are reported to have sold their tariff refund claims to non-bank financial institutions for 40-50 cents on the dollar. Such a ruling may weigh on US yields, as a couple of rating agencies had cited the tariff revenue as credit positive. Although the House of Representatives approved a measure that would rescind the 25% emergency tariff on Canada over fentanyl, it seems largely symbolic. Even if it is approved by the Senate, the president will veto it.

US

Drivers: The market remains sensitive to any confirmation of the so-called debasement trade. News that Chinese officials are encouraging de-risking from US Treasuries hit the dollar hard. However, the unexpected strength in the January employment data helped steady the greenback. Still, after the CPI figures, the futures market is fully discounting two rate cuts this year and little more than a 50% chance of a third. The odds of a June cut, possibly the first that Warsh chairs, have eased to almost 85% from around 97% at the end of last week. Eight Fed officials speak this week, but the market seems to be more aggressive than most.

Data: It is a packed week for US data and is squeezed into four sessions due to Monday's holiday. The highlights include what will likely be soft January retail sales after the large drop in vehicle sales, an increase in manufacturing output despite the continued loss of jobs, and the first estimate of Q4 data. The Atlanta Fed's GDP tracker is 3.7% growth, while the median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for 2.8%. Lastly, the December core PCE deflator is expected to have risen to 2.9% at the headline level from 2.8% in November.

Prices: At last week's lows, the Dollar Index retraced (61.8%) of its rally off the late January low. After the midweek low, DXY remained confined to Wednesday's range (~96.50-97.25). The 96.20-40 area may cap the upside. The momentum indicators are mixed, perhaps warning of continued consolidation in the coming days.

EMU

Drivers: US considerations seem to be the key force in moving the exchange rate. European developments seem scarce and steady. The ECB is widely seen on a protracted hold. There is a small bias in the swaps market toward a cut, but the odds are no more than around 15% through H1. The correlation changes in the exchange rate and interest rates (US two-year yield, German two-year yield, and the differential) are hovering near zero.

Data: With the eurozone's initial estimate of 0.3% quarter-over-quarter growth in Q4 25, matching the Q3 pace, the remaining December data, which this week includes industrial output, the current account, and construction output, is of passing interest. Germany's ZEW February survey is due February 17, but it rarely has much market impact. That leaves the highlight of the week, the flash February PMI on Friday. As of January, the manufacturing PMI was still below the 50 boom/bust level, while the service PMI was at 51.6. The composite has been above 50 since the end of 2024.

Prices: The euro rose to start the week but remained mostly within Monday's range (~$1.1810-$1.1925) for the rest of the week. The 20-day moving average is around $1.1840, and the euro has not closed below it in nearly a month. The euro's bounce stalled near the halfway point of the losses from the January 27 high (~$1.2080) and the February 6 low ($1.1765). The momentum indicators are mixed and not particularly helpful now.

China

Drivers: Chinese officials continue to sanction a gradual appreciation of the RMB. Beginning last May, the daily reference rate rose in only one month (September). Last week was the 12th consecutive week the fix has been lowered. In fact, a week in late November is the only week that the fix has risen since the end of September. This seems to be more a signal that some estimate that the state-owned banks are intervening on behalf of the PBOC.

Data: Chinese markets are closed from February 16 through February 23 for the Lunar New Year.

Prices: With no onshore yuan trading next week and near its best levels in several years, the market may be reluctant to push the offshore yuan far from where it was trading when the onshore market closed (~CNH6.9065). The dollar's range last week was roughly CNH6.8910-CNH6.9330.

Japan

Drivers: The market was positioned for an LDP victory in the February 8 election. The outcome was anticipated to be good for Japanese equities and negative for JGBs and the yen. While the equity call was correct, the JGBs and yen were bought, not sold. It turned out that lower yields did not prevent yen strength. Market positioning seems to be a more important driver of the exchange rate than interest rates presently. The rolling 30-day correlation between changes in the dollar-yen exchange rate and the Dollar Index bottomed last month near 0.50 and reached 0.80 last week, the highest since last October. It is now a little below 0.75.

Data: The busy week for Japanese economic data kicks off with the first look at Q4 25 GDP early Monday. The world's third-largest economy is expected to have grown by around 0.5% in the quarter after it contracted by 0.6% in Q3. Consumption seems to have remained soft, but business spending likely expanded, and net exports may have contributed in a small way after it shaved 0.2% off Q3 GDP. The January trade balance is due, and it has deteriorated without fail at the start of the year. It recorded a surplus of JPY113.5 bln in December. At the end of the week, Japan's January CPI will be reported shortly before the preliminary February PMI. We already know from the Tokyo figures released in late January that moderating food and energy prices will likely drag the headline rate below 2.0% for the first time since March 2022. The market typically has a muted response to the PMI series, but for the record, Japan's January composite PMI was 53.1.

Prices: In what appears to be a classic "sell the rumor, buy the fact" type of activity, the yen defied expectations after Takaichi led the LDP to a historic victory and rallied. In fact, last week's approximately 2.8% advance was the yen's biggest weekly rally since November 2024. The dollar found support near JPY152.25, a little above last month's low (~JPY152.10). The momentum indicators warn of the risk of further dollar losses, but we are less convinced. A break could signal a move toward the JPY150.80 area, which hosts the (61.8%) retracement of the dollar's rally from last September 17, when the Fed delivered its first of three rate hikes in 2025, and the 200-day moving average, which the dollar has not settled below since October 3. A move above JPY154.50 may be an early indication that a low is in place.

UK

Drivers: The immediate risk that UK Prime Minister Starmer is forced out has receded, and that appears to have taken some pressure off sterling, as an idiosyncratic factor. The dollar seems to be driving the exchange rate. The 30-day rolling correlation between changes in sterling and the Dollar Index is around -0.80. This year's range has been about -0.70 to -0.85. The 30-day correlation of changes in sterling and the UK's two-year yield is near 0.20, and correlation with the US two-year yield is only slightly lower.

Data: The three most market-significant economic reports are due this week: jobs, CPI, and retail sales. Government finances (January budget) and the preliminary February PMI also will be released. The dovish hold by the Bank of England on February 5 saw the market boost the odds of a hike at the next meeting (March) to a little more than 70% from about 20% before the meeting. Any weakness in prices and/or activity will likely lift the chances.

Prices: After jumping from about $1.35 to $1.37 in two sessions (February 6 and February 9), sterling traded mostly sideways. It had the dubious honor of being the worst G10 currency last week, rising by about 0.20% against the dollar and falling by about 0.25% against the euro. It briefly traded at a four-day low ahead of the weekend, a little below $1.3600. A close below $1.3600 weakens the technical tone, while it may take a move above $1.3730 to indicate an upside break.

Canada

Drivers: The rolling 30-day correlation of change in the Dollar Index and in the US dollar against the US dollar has approached 0.85, the highest since June 2024. The inverse correlation of the exchange rate changes and Canada's two-year yield is about -0.42. Last March, it recorded 2025 extreme near -0.65. It reached almost +0.40 in early October 2025. The 30-day correlation of changes in the exchange rate and the US two-year note is near zero. It has not been above 0.20 since last October.

Data: Canada saw the foreign appetite for its stocks and bonds wane last year as the merchandise trade deficit grew three-fold. The December updates are due this week. Through November, the merchandise trade deficit stood at C$30.6 bln compared with C$8.9 bln in the first 11 months of 2024. Foreign portfolio net inflows were about C$121 bln in the January-November period, down from C$179 bln in the comparable 2024 period. Canada also reports December retail sales, coming after a 1.3% surge in November (1.7%) excluding auto sales. The January CPI is on tap as well. Headline inflation finished last year at 2.4%, with the core at 2.5%. Bank of Canada Governor Macklem has suggested that the underlying core measures (median and trimmed mean at 2.5% and 2.7%, respectively) may be exaggerated. The Bank of Canada is widely understood to be on hold, and the bar to another cut seem high.

Prices: The US dollar recorded a bullish outside up day in the middle of last week as it recovered from a test on CAD1.3500. Follow-through buying lifted it to almost CAD1.3640 ahead of the weekend. This largely met the (61.8%) retracement objective of the decline from the February 6 high (~CAD1.3725) to the midweek low. The 20-day moving average is near CAD1.3660, and the greenback has not settled above it since January 21. A move above it could signal a return to the CAD1.3750 area.

Australia

Drivers: The bull case for the Australian dollar is predicated on the prospects of tighter monetary policy, as a G10 proxy for China, and its exposure to commodities, especially the metals. The rolling 30-day correlation with changes in gold is near 0.80, the highest in two years. It is around 0.48 with copper. It peaked last June at twice as much. The inverse correlation with the Dollar Index is around -0.70, the most extreme since last October. The correlation of changes in the Aussie and Australia's two-year yield is a little below 0.25. It has not been above 0.40 for nearly three years. The correlation with changes in the two-year US yield is a little above 0.40. Except for a brief period last month, the counterintuitive positive correlation has been the case since the middle of last October. Last year, it was inverted in January and February and was positively correlated between March and late May, when it became inverted until early Q4.

Data: The minutes from the recent meeting at which the central bank lifted the target rate by 25 bp will be reported early Tuesday, followed by Q4 wage data the following day. On Thursday, the January employment data will be reported. In 2025, Australia's full-time positions increased by an average of 8.3k a month. In 2024, the average was about 22.3k a month. The unemployment rate stood at 4.1% in December 2025 and 4.0% in December 2024. The participation rate eased to 66.7% at the end of 2025 from 67.1% at the end of 2024. Lastly, at the end of the week, the flash February PMI will be released. Recall that in January, the composite PMI jumped to 55.7 from 51.0, the highest since April 2022, with new orders and employment rising. Separately, the Reserve Bank of New Zealand meets early on February 18. Its easing cycle is over, and the swaps market has a hike fully discounted by the end of October.

Prices: The Australian dollar peaked on February 12, slightly shy of $0.7150, a three-year high. Profit-taking kicked in, and the Aussie fell to about $0.7045 before the weekend. The momentum indicators are mixed. Chart support is seen in the $0.7000-20 area. It would seem that much of the good news has been discounted in the Aussie's nearly 6% rally in the first half of Q1. The four-week rally in tow is the longest since June-July 2024.

Mexico

Drivers: The dollar remains stuck in its trough against the Mexican peso. It is generally true for many Latam currencies, including the Brazilian real, and the Colombian and Chilean pesos. Two shared characteristics stand out: relatively high rates and commodity exposure. The Mexican peso may also benefit from mostly global liquidity (admittedly it is not evenly distributed) and relatively low volatility. Over the past 30 sessions, changes in the US dollar against the peso are more inversely correlated with the JP Morgan Emerging Market Currency Index than the MSCI Emerging Market Currency Index (~-0.80 vs. -0.38). To dollar-based investors, between the yield pickup and the peso's gains, the return so far this year is about 5.7%. It is 4.7% for euro-based investors and 3.3% for yen-based investors.

Data: There are two notable reports in the coming days, and both are due in the second half of the coming week. On Thursday, the record from the recent Banxico meeting that seemed to confirm an extended pause in policy will be reported. On Friday, December retail sales will be announced. In November, they rose by an unexpectedly sharp 1.0%. That helped lift the average gain in the first 11 months of 2025 to 0.4%, compared with a 0.1% decline in the January-November 2024 period.

Prices: The dollar remained stuck in its trough against the Mexican peso last week. It traded in a range of about MXN17.1265 to MXN17.2820. The high was recorded ahead of the weekend. Given the strength of the yen and the resilience of the peso, it appears that the long peso/short yen carry trades were already adjusted. President Trump reportedly indicated he is considering ending the USMCA, which he negotiated in his first term and had said it was the best trade deal ever. The market hardly reacted as it looked more like a negotiating ploy. Continued consolidative trading, even if choppy, seems to be the most likely near-term scenario.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Microsoft's Lack Of Leadership In AI, Wait
data.description:
Investment thesis

I recommend holding Microsoft (MSFT) because it is an AI player that hasn’t proved an advantage over its peers, as even though we haven't seen much momentum with Copilot, the aggressive data center build-out is still happening. In my view, that infrastructure investment is actually logical. The company is stuck in a mature business, personal computing, that won’t support growth. It is not overvalued at $443.7 per share, but with a small margin compared to the risk it faces with tough competition and possible disruption in the software market, I recommend holding. I estimate a probability of 56.8% of getting a value greater than the current stock price.

High CAPEX expenditures: is it bad?

CAPEX has been high at $29.9 billion in the last quarter, much higher than the $15.8 billion in the same quarter last year. Microsoft's CAPEX-to-revenue has risen from 23% a year ago to 37% in the last quarter. A lot of discussion has been around the CAPEX investment in the hyperscalers, led by Michael Burry. However, I question this thesis. First of all, I always consider free cash flow to be the main measure of value, which eliminates accounting maneuvers around amortization.

The Intelligent Cloud segment grew 28.8% last quarter over the same period last year to reach $32.9 billion in revenue. That is fine. However, it is also nice to see that margins in this segment are flat at 42.2%, as in this growth area, consolidates a financial discipline mentality.

I think it is an error to consider only the Intelligent Cloud segment when analyzing AI. We must analyze the Productivity and Business Processes too, where revenue growth is 15.9%, and margins are expanding from 57.4% to 60.4% due to incremental revenue growth due to Copilot. So, my perspective is that you have to see the benefits in the whole company and not only in the segment associated with CAPEX investments because the Microsoft model is integrated around AI.

The useful life of GPUs and server equipment is 2-2.5 years, regardless of what amortization says. And about 2/3 of CAPEX is in GPUs and server equipment. Management says that “a lot of the GPUs that we're buying are already contracted for most of their useful life.” It is hard to believe that these two things are not happening or are going to happen: 1) the real lifetime value of the client is greater than RPO reflects, 2) GPUs are useless in these 2-2.5 years. In Q2 2026, RPO increased to $625 billion, and its duration of 2.5 years would mean an annual churn rate of 40% that is hard to believe in services like Microsoft’s, so its customer lifetime value must be greater than the contracts signed so far.

The physical life of a GPU can be one to three years. Due to its high use (60-70% utilization), the chip will become unstable, causing calculation errors. We could assume that even before the GPU starts to malfunction, energy costs will be so high that they wouldn’t compensate for the revenue it generates. I estimate that in two years the GPU’s performance won’t be economically profitable. CAPEX in the last 12 months trailing is $83 billion, and two-thirds of them are for GPUs or related equipment in data center infrastructure. If RPO is $625 billion (Figure 1), with a 2.5 mean contract duration, revenue per year would be $125 billion, which, with a gross margin of 68.6%, the annual benefit from this investment is $85 billion per year. So, the payback is less than one year.

Copilot monetization is evolving, but not spectacularly

The company has 15 million in paid seats with Copilot, which means a 5.4 billion revenue run-rate. It is composed of a diversified revenue stream that comes from Microsoft 365 Copilot Subscriptions (40%), Azure AI Consumption (28%), GitHub Copilot (18%), Copilot Pro & Windows Integration (9%), and Enterprise AI Customization & Consulting (5%). ARPU in the M365 Commercial Cloud grew a solid 9.6%: 17% revenue growth and 6% user growth. Customers with more than 35,000 seats have tripled, GitHub Copilot has almost 5 million paid subscribers, which is about a 75% growth rate year over year in the quarter.

On the other hand, 15 million paid seats are just 3.3% of total paid seats, which are 450 million across all customer segments. This is a poor number after almost three years since the AI boom started by OpenAI (OPENAI), with the financial support from Microsoft. I really expected a faster evolution, and they are not getting the buzz other companies are getting. That is also an opportunity, as the company’s management is able to accelerate this penetration in the coming years.

Some disappointment in the rest of the services

The More Personal Computing segment decreased 2.7% to $14.3 billion in the last quarter compared to the same period last year. It is notorious that there is a 9.5% decrease in Gaming, and a 0.7% decrease in Windows and Devices. LinkedIn was decent at a 10.8% growth rate at $5 billion and 7.1% in Search and news advertising growth, but this segment weighs 18% of total revenue and is currently dragging down results from Microsoft. If Microsoft were to sell this segment, revenue would grow at 21.9%, higher than the current 15.9%, and the operating income margin would be 51.4% compared to 47.1% with the current scope.

Valuation

In my valuation methodology, I discount projected free cash flows at a convenient WACC. I define cash margin as net income adjusted for non-cash items such as amortization and depreciation, stock-based compensation, and deferred income tax, offering a clearer view of operational cash generation than standard margin metrics (Figure 3).

Revenue growth in a conservative scenario could be 13% closer to the historical values. The increase in revenue due to AI efforts is partially erased by the Personal Computing segment that is facing cyclical headwinds, but with no high-growth prospects. I estimate the cash margin will be deteriorating along the years. From 55% in 2026 to 51% in 2035 due to difficulties in maintaining prices in an environment with tough competitors and technologies that erode the value of software. I see a gradual decrease in CAPEX investment over the years because AI infrastructure technology matures and providers face a commodity market. Besides, I foresee AI capacity turning to devices vs. Cloud.

Cash flows will be discounted at 8.5% WACC because the beta is 1.08. The risk-free rate is 4.2%. Debt, as a percentage of market capitalization, is 2%, and I consider it to have no excess cash. The perpetual growth rate is set at 3%, which is slightly better aligned with the long-term GDP growth rate.

I estimate a value for Microsoft stock at $443.7 per share, a premium of 10.6%. Microsoft is in the middle position of hyperscalers and is not a clear opportunity for me. Premium is not high, and there is no price advantage among its peers (Figure 5).

Figure 5

Risks

The first risk is competition in the cloud space. Intense competition could harm revenue due to price erosion or losing market share. As a consequence, margins would decrease due to semi-fixed research and innovation expenses. I don’t assign a high probability of occurrence because the market is almost an oligopoly of a few competitors, and switching costs for a company are high at the moment, as the customer holds its systems in a cloud provider. I estimate the impact as +/- 5 percentage points compared to the base case. And +/- 3 percentage points in cash margin

Low CAPEX monetization is the second risk. In the article, I have outlined the economic dynamics of GPU and AI infrastructure investments. The risk is an excessive churn of the clients, or a too small incremental ARPU for the useful life of the AI infrastructure. This should reduce revenue growth by +/-3 percentage points

Software loses value for clients as they can customize software on their own with the new developer tools, such as Claude Code. This will impact the Productivity segment with a reduction that could be in the range of 10%-20% in the segment’s revenue.

With these uncertainties and using Crystal Ball to execute a Monte Carlo simulation, the probability of getting a value higher than the current stock price is 56.8%.

Conclusion

Microsoft is a solid business; however, in this changing world, some threats are shaking its business model. I believe the AI infrastructure investment is not a bubble and makes business sense, as once you capture the client, switching costs are high, and there are several options to upgrade to new services. Copilot is growing, but at a pace I consider somewhat disappointing. 20% of its business is in a mature market that puts the brakes on growth.

The stock is undervalued at $443.7 per share, with an upside not enough for me to feel comfortable. Risks are more severe than ever, and I recommend caution on the stock.

data.title: SOLT: Not For The Faint Of Heart
data.description:
There was a lot of anticipation concerning the introduction of SOL ETFs in the recent past, but the crypto correction has crushed these ETFs, and more so when considering SOLT 2x Solana ETF (SOLT), which has the aggressive goal of delivering two times the daily performance of Solana (SOL-USD).

From its inception in March 2025, it started off well, jumping from approximately $8.00 per share on April 7, 2025, after a brief decline after it started trading. From there it soared to its abovementioned 52-week high on 9-18-2025, before plummeting to its 52-week low in early February. The extremely volatile ETF has a 52-week range of $2.22 to $35.30.

SOLT has fund AUM of $134 million as of 2-12-2026 and class AUM of $246.5 million. Trading volume stands at 1.75 million.

Interestingly, it pays a small dividend of $0.09, which is paid out monthly, for a 3.71% yield (TTM). It also has a hefty expense ratio of 1.85%.

In this article we'll look at what the future looks like for SOLT and the type of investor it's set up for.

What SOLT is

As I opened the article with, SOLT is an actively managed ETF that is designed to generate 2× the daily return of Solana.

The ETF doesn't hold any of the underlying but utilizes cash‑settled Solana futures contracts and swaps in an attempt to achieve its 2x goal. Included in the fixed income part of the fund is cash, cash‑like instruments, or high‑quality securities for

Being a non-diversified fund means almost all of its performance will be determined by its exposure to Solana futures.

How the fund works is every day of trading it resets its exposure to SOL in order to retain its 2x SOL multiplier.

This is, of course, a two-edged sword. If SOL manages to climb 5% on any given trading day, SOLT has the goal of climbing 10%. On the other hand, if SOL drops 5%, SOLT could drop as much as 10%.

Another factor if you are considering trading SOLT is that there is also the compounding factor if holding over several days or more. Under that scenario, the 2x multiplier can get skewed because of the compounding effect on its performance.

Those shorting SOLT

Shorts have had a big impact on trading volume for SOLT since its inception. It especially took off in October 2025, when it had $39 million in short interest. It jumped to its all-time high of $60 million in November 2025. For three out of the next four months, it had short interest of $36 million, with an exception in January 2026, when it jumped back up to $45 million.

The increase in short interest corresponded with a surge in trading volume, which was in a range of approximately 45 million to 71 million. It stayed close to that range until it dropped to about 23 million right before Christmas. After the New Year it started trading at high levels once again, jumping to about 118 million in the first week of February 2026.

As of January 30, 2026, SOLT had a short interest of 6.59 million shares shorted, reflecting 14.41% of public float. Days to cover was 0.5, according to MarketBeat.

With the combination of the crypto correction, number of shorts, and the purpose of the ETF itself, it has resulted in a significant underperformance, which won't be solved until SOL and other cryptos return to an upward growth trajectory.

ETFs that are leveraged, like SOLT, experience what is called volatility drag or decay. That means there can be huge swings in the price from one extreme to the other, even if, in this case, SOL trades flat.

For these reasons, SOLT isn't the type of holding that should be held for the long term.

What is SOLT good for?

The nature of the 2x ETF is that it can be traded on both sides of the play. Money can be made going long or going short.

Day traders or swing traders are the obvious target of the fund. There is no scenario I can think of that would warrant taking a long-term position in SOLT. Even when you look at the chart showing a nice upward move in the early days of the holding, the volatility is extreme, and there's simply no way of knowing whether or not that would be sustainable.

There is also the psychological effect, where most traders would be more apt to sell in response to a big downward swing in the price. This could easily result in a big loss of capital.

On the other hand, as the chart shows, there is some very significant volatility on both sides of the play, and taking a short or long position has the potential to generate some solid gains for those that don't mind taking some risk associated with volatility.

The ETF has a lot of volume on a daily basis, and it has provided some excellent opportunities to take advantage of that.

My thought is this is probably a better swing trade than anything else, based upon the price swings over a number of days. From the time of its inception to its all-time high, it has several moves that doubled or tripled over a period of four to five weeks.

Now that the fund is trading at about its 52-week low, it is worth putting on a watch list for when cryptos reverse direction. One positive is now that it's trading at $2.20 as I write, there is little concern about a rush of shorts into the fund, at least not in the way they have when it was trading much higher with more room to fall.

When looking at the charts, there have been a few days when it had a decent jump, but the bulk of the movement has historically happened over several days to a month or so. The lower it trades when crypto and SOL recover, the more a trader will get out of the holding.

Conclusion

SOLT is designed for a very specific, narrow investment group, one which targets short-term price movements on either side of the volatility. It can be a potentially lucrative investment for those with the discipline and patience to wait for a favorable entry point - whether it's short or long.

Based upon the price movement on a daily basis, it isn't the most attractive investment for day traders, as the swing in price isn't usually enough to justify the risk.

It's definitely a negative for anyone thinking in terms of holding it for the long term. It's too volatile and unpredictable to do so. The risk significantly increases for those thinking in those terms, even when the temptation comes when it starts to soar again.

Without a doubt, this is a holding for swing traders looking to 2x or 3x their money over a period of a week to a month. There has been a predictable move in that regard, and the volatility is potentially profitable for shorts and longs.

For the average retail investor maybe wanting to dip their feet into SOLT, small position sizes are best when considering risk. There's no hurry. Taking a small position offers a chance to watch how the fund performs when there is skin in the game.

I know there can be regret when engaging in mitigating risk, especially if there is only a small amount of money in play. The tendency under those conditions is to get more aggressive when the next opportunity arises in order to get money thought to be lost because of being too conservative.

Based upon the volatility of SOLT, swing traders looking to 2x their money will probably do well when an attractive price point is at hand.

Again, investors need to be aware that this is a great way to play it short and long, with the opportunity to produce meaningful gains when the ETF price is running or grinding to a halt.

data.title: Fortinet: Preparing The Growth
data.description:
On February 5, Fortinet (FTNT) presented its annual results. Fortinet is a company I have talked about many times, most recently this summer. The stock has achieved a slight outperformance with respect to the index, which is quite commendable considering how hard many market sectors are falling.

Today we will mainly discuss its annual earnings, I will show how Fortinet is immersed in a year of transition (despite having grown sales by 13%) and how if investors have a time horizon of 3 years or more, they will be rewarded with good returns, because the business should reaccelerate and continue to improve in returns and profitability.

Earnings Highlights

2025 has been a year in which products have grown more than services, and even so, EBIT margins have increased (remember that products have a gross margin of 67% vs. 87% for services), which makes this figure even more significant.

Taxes have increased from 14% to 19%; that is why EPS has grown at 7.2%. On the other hand, FCF/Share has grown to 19.6% (365M of CAPEX vs. 379M last year), and we continue to see a negative working capital (cash coming in as long as it grows).

Fortinet continued to be the #1 leader in firewalls with 55% unit market share and the highest product revenue among our cybersecurity competitors.

Unified SASE revenue grew 40%, representing 27% of our total revenue, supporting their belief that Fortinet is the fastest growing SASE leader at scale.

Sovereign SASE enabled enterprises and service providers to deploy SASE in their own data center to meet data privacy, sovereignty, and regulatory compliance requirements. They are seeing strong demand for Sovereign SASE, and none of their major SASE competitors offer a Sovereign SASE solution, making Fortinet's Unified SASE TAM significantly higher than that of their peers.

Third, the proprietary, long-term invested FortiCloud global cloud infrastructure delivers high performance and security at approximately 1/3 the TCO of their competitors.

Finally, they reiterated the medium-term goals outlined at Analyst Day, emphasizing their dedication to outperforming the broader market. This includes revenue CAGR exceeding market growth of 12% and delivering performance aligned with the Rule of 45.

Services revenue grew 12% to $1.21 billion, reflecting lower product revenue in 2024, while services revenue growth was solid at 18% in the fourth quarter. Recall that product sales are a leading indicator of services sales.

If they grow more this year and next year and still maintain margins, it means that from 2027 onwards, services should take the lead again, with the corresponding improvement in margins and returns. That acceleration seems to be already being seen in the Billings.

Gross margin of 81.3% remained stable despite the shift towards product revenues and investments in the expansion of our data center infrastructure. Quite commendable in a year in which products have grown more than services.

A positive effect of this higher increase in product growth has been the improvement in Working Capital (in inventories more precisely). Inventory days were down to 98 vs. 126 in the previous year, and the cash conversion cycle was the best in 3 years.

Guidance: Service revenue in the range of $5.05 billion to $5.15 billion, which at the midpoint represents 11% growth. We expect service revenue growth to accelerate in the second half of 2026, driven by higher product revenue growth in 2025 as a key indicator. Non-GAAP gross margin of 79% to 81%; non-GAAP operating margin of 33% to 36%; non-GAAP earnings per share of $2.94 to $3, assuming a share count of 747 million to 753 million; infrastructure investments of $350 million to $450 million; non-GAAP tax rate of 18%; cash taxes of $350 million to $400 million.

Conference Call

The conference call, as usual, was very interesting.

As they said, AI will eventually displace some software companies, but will create new opportunities for cybersecurity: internal segmentation, AI agent control, and data leakage prevention.

Regarding the RAM pricing: Fortinet maintains ~6 months of inventory, lesson learned from COVID. In-house ASICs help with 5x-10x more performance, for the same cost. Price hikes have been implemented on products with cost pressure and could continue in 2026. The price of these represents between 10% and 20% of the cost of the product:

As Ken mentioned, we are planning to maintain our kind of profitability and gross margins on our products in 2 ways, right? One is by negotiating and making sure we get the components early, but also, we've already raised some prices where we have some component cost pressures, and we will potentially continue to do so throughout the year depending on what the components prices do. Christiane Ohlgart, CFO

In fact, they have specified possible price increases, which demonstrate their pricing power.

In general, I believe it is between 5% and 20%. In fact, we can adjust prices on a monthly basis with 30 days' notice. Christiane Ohlgart, CFO

No cannibalization between SASE and product; SASE is increasing market size. They have also been optimistic about the hardware cycle, since, as they said, in the last cycle 5-6 years ago, they did not have as many solutions to upsell as they do now.

Also, the secure networking and Unified SASE can be a leading indicator for some future SecOps because they to tend buy the product first and then eventually will also handle the additional like operational service. Ken Xie, CEO.

They may be a little more open to M&A, but they are still very cautious:

I think the market is a little bit more reasonable now. I think, definitely, there's more opportunity when we look at the merger and acquisition. But we do have the discipline, which we maintained in the last 25 years, kind of tend to acquire the technology or some talent instead of try to buy some market or customer base. Ken Xie, CEO.

Anyway, what's strange to me about this year is that with $2.4B of buybacks and only $280M of SBC, they only decreased shares outstanding by -0.9%.

Valuation

At the current price of around $80, the market would be discounting 12% growth in its FCF going forward. The assumptions I have used are an exit multiple of 25x and 2% per year in buybacks, without dividends.

I believe that FCF growth will be closer to 15%, since the future increment of services should bring higher margins and returns for the company, plus Fortinet has been immersed in an expansive CAPEX cycle for a few years to build its own servers and not rely entirely on third parties. This growth would leave a fair value of $92.

For all these reasons, I rate the stock as a buy, but let me clarify one thing.

Currently, the market is moving only on narratives and not on what the underlying businesses will do 3-5 years out. Right now it only rewards momentum, a momentum that is not great in Fortinet's case, but not that bad compared to other sectors such as SaaS.

At a graphic level, Fortinet is in a clear support zone that has been very important for several years. It would be essential not to lose $73. If it rises above $88, it would trigger a double bottom with a target of $100.

Risks

The main risk I see with Fortinet is more of a collective or systemic one. As I’ve mentioned before, for a generalist investor, it’s very difficult to follow and fully understand all the innovations and disruptions constantly taking place among companies in this space. Even though Fortinet appears to be the clear leader in its niche, you never know when the next big revolution might emerge.

That’s why I feel comfortable relying on the deep expertise of Fortinet’s management team, which has executed brilliantly since the company’s founding in 2000.

However, this also creates a key-man risk, which falls directly on the shoulders of the founders, Ken Xie and Michael Xie. We’ve already seen a warning sign this year with the retirement of the CFO back in Q2 — a development that shouldn’t be overlooked.

"And on a personal note, you may have read in today's 8-K filing that after a 4-decade career in finance, including 11 years at Fortinet, it's time for me to enjoy retirement. I'll continue to serve through the next quarter earnings call and up to May 15 and plan to stay at Fortinet to help with the transition through June 30.

Most importantly, I'm leaving Fortinet in very good hands. Pursuant to our succession plan, Christiane Ohlgart, who as discussed in the 8-K, has served in various roles at Fortinet for almost 6 years, will take over as CFO at my step down in May." Keith Jensen, Ex-CFO.

This reminds us that the day will eventually come when the founders themselves will also retire. A CEO transition is never easy, but when it involves a founder who has been at the helm for 25 years, it becomes even more complex. There has been no mention of when this might happen, but it's a risk looming on the horizon—one that will inevitably materialize at some point.

Conclusions

I think it has not been a bad year at all for Fortinet, in fact, we have seen it in its strong cash generation per share (+19.6%). In the long term, this is what dictates the share price, and without a doubt, the intrinsic value of Fortinet as a company has grown this year.

2026 will continue to be a year in which products will continue to grow faster than services, but let's not forget that these are just the Trojan horse to then upsell and cross-sell services with higher recurrence and margin. Fortinet will emerge stronger from this hardware expansion cycle going forward.

data.title: ASML: EUV Orders Explode, The Setup Into 2026-2028 Just Improved
data.description:
ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) has gained 5.6% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500, which remained more or less flat. The world’s biggest manufacturer of lithography reported mixed Q4 earnings, like in Q3. In this report, I discuss ASML’s Q4 2025 results, the outlook for 2026 and I update my price target.

No Surprises For ASML In Q4

Sequentially, ASML’s sales rose from €7.5 billion to €9.7 billion, marking 29% growth, with most of the growth being driven by net system sales. Installed base management sales rose just 5%, which is not surprising as these recurring revenues add aftermarket sales over time. So, high growth in net system sales adds growth for installed base management over a longer time period. Gross margins increased 60 basis points to 52.2%, while operating margins increased 7.2 points to 35.3%. Net income margins rose 90 basis points to 29.2%, while earnings per share rose from €5.49 to €7.35. Bookings surged from €5.4 billion to €13.2 billion, including €7.4 billion in EUV bookings, compared to €3 billion a year ago and €3.6 billion in the prior quarter. This is a strong marker for growth into 2027-2028.

Compared to the same quarter last year, total sales increased 4.3%, fully driven by net system sales, which grew 7%. Gross margins improved 50 basis points to 52.2%, while operating margins decreased 90 basis points to 35.3% on higher R&D and net income margins improved 10 basis points to 29.2%. Earnings per share increased 7.3% to €7.35. Bookings surged 86%, with a 150% increase in EUV bookings.

What we are seeing is that AI continues to drive demand for lithography machines, including and predominantly for EUV machines. At the same time, we note that looking at the sequential performance can be done for the first three quarters of the year, but the fourth quarter tends to be the strongest quarter, making the sequential comparisons less meaningful. If we look at the year-on-year performance, we note that sales growth is not necessarily impressive at just 4.3%

By geographic region, we note that sales to China accounted for 36%, which is a sequential step down from 42% in Q3 as EUV technology sales ramped up and China cannot procure this type of advanced lithography tooling. Logic end-use accounted for 70% of sales, with the remaining 30% going to Memory. What is interesting to note is that most revenues still are for CPU, GPU and AI accelerator end-use. High Bandwidth Memory has been a key bottleneck, and we also see SSD demand surging, but in the revenue footprint, the memory end-use is rather small, and perhaps that would also drive growth in future quarters and years.

We already see a potential acceleration in the bookings, with 56% of the bookings in Q4 being for memory end-use, and the €38.8 billion backlog now consisting of 40% of memory end-use orders compared to 32% a year ago. So, manufacturers are stepping up their investments in the Memory space, and we also note that Memory revenues already accelerated in 2024.

ASML Guidance Shows Backloaded Delivery Profile

For the first quarter, ASML is guiding for revenues between €8.2 billion and €8.9 billion, including €2.4 billion in Installed Base Management sales. This indicates year-on-year growth of 11%, and that puts a marker for the full year, which is guided at €34-€39 billion in sales. Gross margins are guided at 51%-53%, with first-quarter R&D and SGA guided at a total of €1.5 billion.

In 2026, EUV revenues will be up significantly with flat non-EUV sales and higher Installed Base Management revenues. The driver for EUV revenues is the 2nm node transition and high NA EUV entering high-volume manufacturing. In non-EUV, we note that China is expected to account for 20% of revenue, while metrology and inspection sales are expected to drive 30% growth. The company also announced that its 2025 dividend will be €7.50, marking 17% growth, while its €12 billion repurchase program, which had €4.4 billion left in authorized repurchases, has been replaced with a new €12 billion program running through 2028. The company aims for €44-€60 billion in sales by 2030 with gross margins of 56%-60%, driven by structural growth of application of EUV and high NA demand driving equipment as well as aftermarket sales.

ASML Buy Rating Remains As 2027 Earnings Expectations Improved

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For ASML, I am increasing my price target to $1,618 from $1,395, indicating 15% upside. The increase is driven by EBITDA estimates increasing 11.5% and 14.4% for 2026 and 2027, driving a total EBITDA lift of 9.9% between 2025 and 2027. Similarly, free cash flow estimates improved by 20.9%, driven by higher volumes and a better cash flow mix of revenues.

If we look at the margins, we see that a strong increase in margins is expected, driven by higher EUV volumes and margins. High NA shipments will also increase, which should ultimately lead to margins maturing and becoming less dilutive to the overall margin profile of ASML. For 2026, free cash flow is expected to dip, which is partially driven by higher CapEx, as ASML also has to increase its production capacity to continue driving delivery volumes higher, and the other side of the dip likely is the high NA shipments, which may come with a less attractive cash profile for the time being. Previously, I noted that ASML was no longer expecting to complete its €12 billion share repurchase program, and I modeled in a new program to be executed in 2026-2027, which would also be around €12 billion, and those expectations have remained unchanged as the company did announce a new $12 billion program for 2026-2028. The company has an extra year in the program, but they actually don't need the extra year from a cash perspective. The cash position remains strong with a small dip in 2026 and the company remains in a net cash position.

Conclusion: ASML Earnings Did Not Blow Me Away, But Upside Remains

The fourth quarter earnings did not blow me away, but we note that orders are looking encouraging, and whereas there had been double-digit growth in 2026, the expectation is currently that ASML, will book double-digit growth in 2026, while estimates for 2027 have also increased. AI is the rather obvious driver for ASML and that is also the biggest risk for the company. Any change in AI investments could ultimately hurt ASML, but we note that AI is still at a stage where its true potential is not yet leveraged, in my view. That also means that return on AI CapEx may be a problem for hyperscalers, but more serious applications with a deeper embed of AI should show the transforming capabilities of AI and remove doubt about the return on investment. I believe that in the years ahead, we will see more focus on a deeper embedding of AI that should support investments. As a result, I maintain my buy rating for ASML.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: HCI Group: Growth Plateauing, Hold At Current Levels
data.description:
Thesis

In my view, HCI Group (HCI) is a competitive and long-standing Florida-centric insurer in an increasingly challenging residential insurance market. They frequently showcase stronger underwriting performance compared to other coastal peers and the broader P&C market. This has been particularly evident in the past few years through improving loss ratios and expense ratios. This results in periods of above-industry average ROE, such as the recent few years, coupled with strong growth in intrinsic value.

However, at present I think HCI should be rated as a hold. Their out-of-state growth story is propped up by their software and data solutions subsidiary, Exzeo Group, whose scalability into other markets remains to be proven. Residual income modelling and relative valuation metrics suggest that the stock is overvalued. This is despite a drop of about 33% in price since November 2025, which may be due to investors not seeing much upside from those price levels after the good but atypical underwriting results in late 2025, cementing their profits from a 3-year run.

Business Snapshot

The HCI Group is primarily a residential coastal insurer based in Florida and founded in 2006. Through their subsidiary, Homeowners Choice Property & Casualty (HCPI) they are authorized to sell insurance in Northeast and Southeast states, though Florida typically makes up about 90% of their Gross Premiums Earned (GPE) while the rest comes from outside states. Their total revenue is roughly composed of Net Premiums Earned (NPE) (90%), Net Investment Income (8%), and the remaining 2% comes from a mixture of their real estate portfolio, software solutions, policy fee income, and realized investment gains.

Underwriting Performance

Effective underwriting discipline is a key driver of long-term price returns for an insurance company. Compared to the broader P&C industry and a peer set of established and impressive coastal insurers, HCI stands out for better-than-average underwriting performance. As can be seen in the graphs below, HCI usually boasts materially lower loss ratios than the P&C average and in recent years has outpaced their established competitors in terms of their combined ratio. The combined ratio is the sum of the loss ratio and expense ratio, which tells us about their underwriting quality and operational efficiency, respectively. Over the past decade, they have often posted lower combined ratios than long-standing peers like HRTG and UVE, reaching levels of the aggressive Slide (SLDE) that I wrote about before. This implies the existence of a competitive advantage that structurally supports their underwriting profitability over peers; this is particularly visible outside of catastrophe years like 2017 and 2022.

Combined Ratio Comparison With Peers

We can dive into their loss and expense ratios to better understand this strength of theirs. Their loss ratios are frequently below coastal peers and the P&C industry. This loss control performance appears to be best-in-class and tells us that their underwriting discipline and risk selection are highly effective. Their losses have been more volatile than peers at times. This is based on larger spikes in 2017 and 2022 from Hurricanes Irma and Ian, respectively. Fortunately, they've shown faster recoveries post-event while clearly suffering from larger spikes in losses than others, but their ability to recover immediately after appears to be stronger since peers' experiences either delayed spikes or elevated losses for a period after.

Furthermore, HCI has demonstrated large improvements in their cost structure in recent years, which improves their margins. Historically they've had a heavier cost base than peers; that's been higher by 5-15 points. But through a combination of reducing interest expenses and acquiring policies from Citizen's depopulation program, they've seen significant cost compression, giving them better expense ratios than HRTG and UVE. For example, in October 2025, they assumed 47,000 policies, which resulted in $181 million in annualised premiums. Assuming policies this way allowed them to grow their gross premiums earned, which has spread their fixed costs over a larger number of policies, and this has saved them expenses on acquiring policies organically. All of this has culminated in strong underwriting results in 2025, a non-cat year, with a CR of about 61%, significantly outperforming established competitors.

Loss & Expense Ratio Comparison with Peers

There is a crucial case to be made against continued strong performance. This is due to the increasing frequency and severity of catastrophes both globally and along the Atlantic Seaboard. While this is applicable to other coastal insurers, we saw from previous years that HCI has accepted more volatile losses (larger spikes) than others. 2025 in particular was a milder year in terms of catastrophes, and going forward I'd expect loss activity to be higher, which is more in line with a typical year and the growing trend. Assuming this worsens, we may expect to see greater earnings volatility and worsening margins over the long run.

Although they have undergone geographic expansion and made progress on diversifying their income streams with rental income (via Greenleaf Capital), policy fees, and software products. HCI is, at present, still heavily dependent on the Florida market for most of their revenue, as was mentioned earlier. Florida is the most affected state by increased natural catastrophes like storm activity and the resulting cost inflation. As a result, if we don't see better diversification over the long run, the current vast improvements in loss ratio and expense ratio may be eroded if most of their gains have already been unlocked, making their long-term returns seem less durable.

However, I think these concerns can be at least partially mitigated by their reinsurance agreements and the Florida Hurricane Catastrophe Fund. These agreements act as capital and solvency shields to limit their downside risk from catastrophes. From the graph below, we can see that they consistently cede roughly 30-38% of their premiums in exchange for this. This was calculated from premiums ceded divided by gross premiums earned. They are keeping a stable retention profile across both hard and soft cycles but allowing a degree of flexibility for elevated catastrophe levels. This means that they keep 62-70% of their premiums and associated claims, showing a moderate level of caution for the risks they select. The reinsurance cost allows them to cap their tail losses, allowing them to preserve their margins if catastrophe losses rise acutely. It's also interesting to see UVE shift from a high reinsurance charge to a balance closer to HCI, along with other insurers converging to similar levels.

Compared to HRTG, it seems HCI is willing to accept greater short-term earnings volatility to maximize their profitability. HCI sits in the middle of this peer set, between insurers who pay a relatively high reinsurance charge, like HRTG, vs. aggressive but less cycle-tested startups like Slide. This points to HCI having found an optimal balance between protection and profit over the long run, allowing them to retain enough for meaningful compounding, as we will see in the growth section. Even with continued Florida exposure and worsening catastrophe trends, they can maintain sufficient capital protection while accepting the short-term swings in earnings.

Reinsurance Cost Comparison With Peers

Growth Prospects

HCI has demonstrated accelerated growth for the past few years in the Florida market through net income and book value per share. For example, for the first 9 months of 2025, their gross premiums earned (GPE) reached $904.1 million as compared to $785.7 million in the same period across 2024. While 2025 benefited from favorable weather conditions, hence the sudden spike in net income and book value for the first 9 months of 2025. Even without favourable weather conditions, they typically show good post-event recovery in catastrophe years. This can be evidenced when, in 2022, their diluted EPS ($6.24) shifted to $8.89 in 2024. Between 2022 and 2024 their revenue grew by nearly 50%, from $499.6m to $750.1m. This was facilitated by acquiring Citizens' policies coupled with growth in their subsidiary TypTap (now rebranded to the Exzeo Group) whose GWP extended from $2.5 million in 2016 to $363.6 million by 2023. This growth is technologically enabled by their Exzeo platforms, along with unlocking massive operational leverage gains in recent years. These cost efficiency gains allow additional premiums earned to translate disproportionately into earnings, fueling their growth and compounding. As a result, they offer investors a cycle-tested growth engine to compound ROE at above-industry average levels.

Key Figures Growth

However, I'm concerned about the long-term durability of their insurance growth engine outside of Florida, which makes me doubt their capacity to scale it and diversify. They've had multi-state licenses for many years now but lately seem to struggle to organically grow in other states. For example, outside Florida, their gross premiums earned flatlined, and their non-Florida growth slowed to roughly 2% YoY growth from about $88 million in the first 9 months of 2024 to about $90 million in the same period in 2025. This still makes up a relatively small proportion of the total, 90% of which comes from Florida, as we saw earlier. This implies that their tech-supported underwriting may not be suitable to penetrate more stable markets that don't see the kind of volatility or peaks that Florida experiences.

Also, much of that volume from other states was inorganic, as it came from assuming policies from United Insurance Holdings in the Northeast and Southeast rather than organic customer acquisition. Despite approval to write in up to 31 states through Typtap (now Exzeo), their plateauing growth may suggest their specialty in Florida-centric risk selection is challenged by the differences in states. Their risk selection may be too specialized for Florida, putting them at a disadvantage in other states. As a result, I'd struggle to see them replicating their Florida success in other states and displacing established insurance carriers in those markets.

On the other hand, their long-term growth may see a beneficial shift from scaling their premiums written across states to scaling their software platforms for backend operations. Their subsidiary, Typtap Group, was remade into the Exzeo Group as a technology-focused, majority-owned subsidiary. It IPO'ed at $21 a share in November 2025, bringing in $168 million in gross proceeds. The insurance side of Typtap was combined into HCPI while their proprietary tech for risk selection and operational leverage was placed in Exzeo. Their suite of cloud-based applications for backend operations includes Harmony (Policy Administration), SAMS, and ClaimsColony (Claims Automation). Before intracompany eliminations, Exzeo's revenue accounts for about $165 million for the first 9 months of 2025. This amounted to 16.6% of total revenue from all operating segments. This backend has contributed significantly to their own revenue, likely playing a role in compressing their expense ratio and partially their loss ratio these past few years. Coupled with the fact that it was forged in one of the most difficult catastrophe markets, there is reason to believe it will be a highly scalable growth engine through being licensed to third-party insurers. Even though premium growth in other states slows, their software licenses may serve as a vehicle to indirectly benefit from insurers' operations in all 50 states. A revenue model like this would be more stable and serve the purpose of diversification from Florida and insurance policies. As a result, HCI may be poised to transition from a strong regional insurer to an insurtech with a scalable path to durable profitability.

Valuation

Multi-Stage Residual Income Model

Running a multi-stage residual income model (RIM) shows that the mean intrinsic value per share of HCI is about $143. The stock price as of the 12th of Feb 2026 is $160, suggesting it is moderately overvalued by about 10%. To secure a position at an optimal price, investors should wait for a pullback, such as during/after a stormy season, or for intrinsic value to catch up.

Price Range

The intrinsic value per share is the sum of the current book value per share, the present value of residual income, and the present value of the terminal value. The residual income is the difference between net income and the minimum required return an investor expects for each forecast year. The terminal value is the sum of residual income earned in perpetuity from year 15 onwards. The book value per share is the assets minus liabilities, i.e., shareholders' equity, on a per-share basis.

Composition of Intrinsic Value:

Components

Intrinsic Value

% of total Intrinsic Value

Book value per share

$82.11

57%

Sum of PV of residual income

$61.24

43%

PV of terminal value

$0.24

Total

$143.59

100%

An investor's required minimum return, aka cost of equity, is calculated using the CAPM formula, Ra=Rrf+[Ba x (Rm-Rrf)]. This formula uses the risk-free rate, equity risk premium, and the beta, which I assume to be 1. The risk-free rate used is the US 10-year treasury yield of 4.129% as of early Feb 2026. The US equity risk premium of 5%, as of Sep 2025, is the extra return an investor expects for accepting the risks of equities over the risk-free asset. The beta of 1.14 tells us how volatile a stock is relative to the market. Greater than one means it is more volatile than the market and vice versa. If it is 1, then it moves in perfect sync with the market on average. Inputting these values gives me the cost of equity of 9.83%, which will be used to discount the residual income and terminal value to get their present values.

CAPM Inputs

I chose a persistence factor of 0.59; this is the decay rate of the excess returns. I thought it was reflective of their Florida-centric specialty and may deserve to be raised depending on the near-term performance of Exzeo Group. The persistence factor does not have a significant effect on the intrinsic value. Their dividend yield (FWD) is low at 0.97%, so it doesn't strongly factor into the growth of their book value. Out of these assumptions, the terminal ROE has the greatest impact on the intrinsic value estimate; I set it at the P&C industry ROE of 10%. A 10% terminal ROE, which I think is conservative, means I assume ROE steadily declines from about 32% in Q3 2025 to 10% across a 15-year horizon. This implies that the firm's ability to create value normalizes to the industry average over time. I wanted to use conservative figures to see if an undervaluation could still be spotted through residual income modelling.

Assumptions Table:

Assumption

Relative Measures:

Relative valuation measures largely support an intrinsic overvaluation. Capital-based metrics show this clearly; e.g., HCI's P/B (FWD) is at 2.2x, which is notably higher than its peer set, who average 1.8x, and the sector median at 1.21x. Overvaluation is corroborated by their costliness on earnings multiples. E.g. GAAP P/E (FWD) of 8.09x is materially higher than their peer set, who average 5.48x. In this case, expansion of valuation multiples based on book value and earnings seems limited, which aligns with the residual income model's conclusion. I think this shows the market already prices in HCI's effective underwriting, post-event recovery, and ability to compound capital, which have been especially clear in post-2022.

Peer Set's Relative Measures Table
